summary: Gruber follows up on his Antennagate analysis with three points: the iPhone 4's higher dropped call rate may partly reflect improved reception in previously dead zones, Apple's antenna testing was far more rigorous than the popular 'they missed it because of cases' theory suggests, and Apple's $175 million free case giveaway was the right move but was undermined by Jobs mixing in media criticism. He argues the iPhone 4 antenna behaved exactly as Apple expected, and that Apple wisely went all-in with their response rather than dribbling out piecemeal answers. However, he faults Jobs for antagonizing the media at the very press conference designed to win them over.
key_points: The iPhone 4's higher dropped call rate may paradoxically indicate improvement — connecting in areas where previous iPhones couldn't connect at all, then occasionally dropping those calls

Apple invested over $100 million in antenna testing facilities with 17 anechoic chambers and 18 PhD engineers, thoroughly debunking the theory that they missed the antenna issue due to case-disguised prototypes
Apple's free case program was the right strategic response to a story spinning out of control, with Letterman jokes, CNN duct tape stories, and daily Consumer Reports updates
Jobs undermined an otherwise effective PR response by mixing customer-focused messaging with media criticism, antagonizing the very people he needed to spread Apple's message
The dropped call problem is not evenly distributed — some users experience significantly more drops while most experience very few
money_quotes: I'll go out and acknowledge that this line of thinking is arguing that the iPhone 4's higher dropped call rate is a good thing, which, on its face, sounds nutty. But is it outlandish?
One thing I'd feel safe betting on: the extra dropped calls from the iPhone 4 are not evenly distributed among all iPhone 4 users. Some are getting a lot, and most are getting very few.
Honestly, I thought the entire point of the lab tour was to reinforce this point: the iPhone 4 antenna is behaving exactly as Apple expected it would.
I think they decided, wisely, that if they were going to hit back in response to the story, they should hit back with everything they had. No use dribbling out responses one at a time.

If the Antennagate PR problem was so dangerous that it warranted a significant response from Apple — and I think it did — then it was dangerous enough that it shouldn't have been mixed with a message that might further antagonize the media.
It doesn't matter whether Jobs was right; it distracted from the core message, which was all about dissipating the meme that the iPhone 4 antenna is severely flawed.
key_insight: Apple's comprehensive Antennagate response was strategically sound, but Jobs weakened it by mixing legitimate customer reassurance with media criticism that antagonized the very outlets he needed on his side.
